`get_active_cloud` takes  a recommended argument of `cli_ctx`.
You can get this by passing `cli_ctx` from the command handler (e.g. `iot_hub_sku_list`) by adding a first argument of `cmd`.

e.g. for `iot_hub_sku_list `:
```
def iot_hub_sku_list(cmd, client, ...):
    server_suffix = _get_device_dns_suffix(cmd.cli_ctx)

def _get_device_dns_suffix(cli_ctx):
    from os import getenv
    from azure.cli.core._profile import get_active_cloud
    return getenv('_AZURE_CLI_IOT_DNS_SUFFIX', default=get_active_cloud(cli_ctx).suffixes.iot_device_hostname)
```

aside is not expected to be related with the main content. from the specs:

> The aside element represents a section of a page that consists of content that is tangentially related to the content around the aside element, and which could be considered separate from that content. Such sections are often represented as sidebars in printed typography.

Also, our portlets are already wrapped in an aside column. But I consider that as not optimal, as the content is only for layout purposes.
Each aside introduces a new section. The examples in the w3 document show grouped "portlets" in an aside tag, but also multiple aside tags for one side bar. Since we don't have a defined semantic relationship between portlets and hardly can establish one, wrapping each portlet into an aside tag sounds good to me.

HTML 5 leaves much room for interpretation. Are there other opinions? /cc @polyester @davilima6 anyone else?

I actually found the root cause, the tutorial was running on CPU and using a slow CPU version of MXNet resulting in 32x slow down compared to the mkl version. fixed it.

The problem being fixed by the PR is that the cpu pushes gpu seed-setting kernels to the engine, then can launch random-number-consuming kernels without synchronization (perhaps to a different stream).  Pushing multiple seed-setting kernels before the ultimate desired one is one way of aggravating this race condition.

On py3 you'll pass a `str` or `bytes` to this function. `bytes` will `.decode` to `str`. py3 `str` is also accepted by `Repository`.

> Oh sorry, I misread. I do think we should provide defaults here. They don't necessarily need to be what we use but otherwise I think the use-case is a bit non-obvious.

I'm not sure how providing quantopian-specific defaults makes the use case more obvious.

As this stands, the most likely outcome of using this function for a non-quantopian user is that they'll end up with empty plots because none of their columns matched a default naming scheme that's tied to our internal risk model implementation.  To fix that problem, the user either has to pass their own partitioning scheme or rename their columns, but renaming is only viable if there's a 1-1 correspondence between their risk model columns and ours, which seems unlikely. So I think the practical outcome for a non-Quantopian user is that they'll have to pass a partitioning scheme no matter what, and by providing our current default we're just making the initial user experience more confusing.

Ultimately I'll defer to your decision on this, but IMO this default amounts to making PyFolio a worse library for non-Quantopian users in exchange for making it more convenient for us to use internally.

In order to Betelgeuse proper map the `@ID` with the jUnit result it is needed that both the test runner and Betelgeuse get called on the same directory. With that said by having an entry like the following on the jUnit XML report:

```xml
<testcase classname="data_sample.tests.test_login_example.LoginTestCase" file="data_sample/tests/test_login_example.py" line="421" name="test_login_1" time="694.768339396">...</testcase>
```

Betelgeuse will map the test method based on its classname and name by joining them with a dot: `data_sample.tests.test_login_example.LoginTestCase.test_login_1` then Betelgeuse will convert every test module path into a python import path for example `data_sample/tests/test_login_example.py` will become `data_sample.tests.test_login_example` and all test methods or functions will then be appended later on, for the method `test_login_1` inside the class `LoginTestCase` it will generate `data_sample.tests.test_login_example.LoginTestCase.test_login_1`. With the two information matching then it will check the `@ID` token of that test method or function and will query Polarion for the work item id that represents that ID, finally with the work_item_id information Betelgeuse will add the result for a Polarion test case (work item) into the test run.
